title: Pillars For Building A Successful Team
summary: Erin Stetzer presented her five pillars for building a high-performing construction team: ownership mindset, clear systems and processes, proactive communication, decision-making authority, and continuous improvement. The session then opened to Q&A where participants asked about extending team culture to subcontractors, hiring first project managers, compensation structures, onboarding PMs onto live projects, and overhead allocation. Sabrina, a team member, also shared her firsthand perspective on what makes Erin an effective leader to work for.

teachings.explanation: Every team member should think and act like they own the company before escalating any issue. When faced with a problem, they should first analyze why it exists and develop at least three potential solutions before bringing it to others. This speeds up operations and reduces bottleneck dependency on the owner.
teachings.quote: When they know they need to own the problem, or the project, or the solution, operationally, it speeds things up. There's an analysis on why there's a problem.

teachings.concept: Clear Systems and Processes as Culture Foundation
teachings.explanation: Systems and processes don't need to be complicated, but they must exist so team members have structure to work within. Consistent practice of systems is what makes them real — a system that isn't practiced isn't actually a system. The owner sets the culture, and systems are the tangible expression of that culture.
teachings.quote: You can have systems and if they're not practiced, it's not a system.

teachings.explanation: Staying two to three steps ahead of everyone at all times is critical. Team members should proactively brief leadership on changes, decisions, and potential issues before they are discovered organically. The goal is that no one walks into a situation surprised.
teachings.quote: She prepped me for it. She knows how I think. That's how teams operate.

teachings.explanation: Owners should define clear financial and operational thresholds within which team members can make autonomous decisions without escalation. This removes fear of judgment, keeps projects moving, and builds team confidence. Owners must back their team's decisions within those agreed parameters.
teachings.quote: She knew ahead of time, like, my max is $2,000. Otherwise, we have to have — I want to have a meeting with Erin.

teachings.explanation: Teams should be constantly asking how to do things better. Leaders model this by reviewing their day each morning, identifying key takeaways, and reaching out to team members with improvement ideas. This mindset should be embedded in team meetings as a recurring agenda item.
teachings.quote: The very next morning, I actually run through the previous day and pick out my key takeaways where I could have done something better.

teachings.concept: Forward Thinking as a Trainable Skill
teachings.explanation: Many problems in construction can be prevented by training team members to think three to four steps ahead at all times. Analyzing downstream consequences of every small decision — like matching a paint color from a different brand — prevents costly cascading mistakes. This is a learned behavior, not an innate trait.
teachings.quote: I don't think this comes naturally to people. I think it's a trained thing that in every little thing, at least here, every little thing we do, we want to think forward three or four steps.

teachings.explanation: Rather than forcing all trades into group communication, identify which trades naturally work well together and love working with each other. Build micro teams around those natural affinities so trades coordinate with each other directly, covering for one another without builder intervention.
teachings.quote: They have to care more about each other than they actually care about the builder. That's what I've learned.

teachings.concept: Studying Plans and Details as the Foundation of Ownership
teachings.explanation: The foundation of ownership mindset is deep familiarity with the project. Team members and owners alike should study plans, specifications, and details so thoroughly that they catch issues before they become problems. Curiosity and asking questions — regardless of experience level — is the differentiator.
teachings.quote: I know nothing to this day. I still know nothing when I walk into a house.

actionable_advice: Require team members to identify at least three potential solutions to a problem before escalating it to you or another team member.
Set clear financial decision-making thresholds for each team member so they can act autonomously without needing owner approval for every decision.
Build micro teams of trades who already enjoy working together — ask each trade who they love working with and consolidate those relationships intentionally.
When onboarding a new project manager, follow behind them on the same day to observe what they notice and what they miss, rather than leaving them fully unsupervised.
Hold Friday afternoon office hours (e.g., 1pm onward) as a structured time for project managers to review open issues rather than allowing constant interruptions throughout the week.
When recruiting top-tier trades who aren't looking for work, use existing vendor relationships to make warm introductions and share proof of your payment reliability and communication systems.
When interviewing candidates, bring real plans and ask technical questions — if you're learning something from them, that's a strong signal they're high quality.
Study plans and material details deeply on every project and use tools like ChatGPT to verify material compatibility and flag potential issues before they arise.
When bonusing project managers, consider quarterly bonuses tied to gross profit margin rather than waiting until project close, since long timelines make end-of-project bonuses demotivating.
Calculate your monthly burn rate including your own salary, then ensure all active projects together cover that burn rate before modeling additional overhead allocation.
Place project manager fees below the cost-of-work line in budgets to clearly distinguish internal versus external costs and ensure the fee is not marked up again incorrectly.
client_challenges: How do you extend ownership culture and communication systems to 1099 subcontractors, especially when trades resist being put in group texts with each other?
When a team member makes a mistake or a project doesn't go as expected, how do you rebuild trust with them and within yourself to move forward without ruminating?
How do you practically develop an ownership mentality in employees beyond just telling them about it?
What are the red flags to watch for when interviewing potential subcontractors or project managers for a long-term relationship?
How do you recruit and attract top 1% performers who are already fully booked and not actively looking for new builders to work with?
How do you hire and onboard your very first project manager when you're newer to the industry and don't yet have a network of PMs?
Should a first PM be a shared/solopreneur contractor working across multiple builders, or should you wait until you can offer full-time exclusive employment?
When bringing on a new PM for a large project, how do you structure their ramp-up without exposing clients to their inexperience?
How do you allocate overhead costs — including non-PM team members like administrative or financial staff — across projects to accurately understand true project profitability?
What are realistic project manager salary ranges across different markets, and how should bonus structures be designed?
notable_quotes: On ownership mindset: 'Good or bad, own it. Take it. Run with it.'
On problems as opportunities: 'Every time a problem comes our way, it's like, all right, what you got? What you got? What you got?'

On proactive communication limits: 'Don't be excessive because you might get yourself in trouble. It's like my boys, when they talk to me — they give me yeses and nos. Just be thoughtful about how you want to communicate.'
On honesty as a non-negotiable: 'If you come in and make up a story, that's just not going to work. I don't care if you make a $500 mistake. If you're telling me the truth, we can work through that.'

On hiring and trust: 'I've always been the type where I'll run myself to the ground until I can't do it anymore, and then I'll bring someone in — but it also sets you up so if you're going to hire someone, you know they're going to be paid well and want to stick around.'

On the human side of team building: 'We're all going to kick it in less than a hundred years, we're out of here. I think a big thing is just really trying to do what we can to help other people get to their goals. And if we get to play in the sand together for a little bit of it, that's even better.'
